days.content: Look for and avoid areas of drifted snow on steep slopes. Two pulses of intense snow with easterly winds increase the danger on Friday. Easterly winds mean you should look out for drifted snow in atypical places and on typically scoured slopes. You can easily trigger avalanches where you find more than about 10 inches of recently drifted snow. Weak surfaces before the storm mean slides might run faster than you expect. Make sure you're aware of what's above and below you. Cracking the new snow is a sign to avoid steep slopes. Seek out wind-sheltered slopes as a safer option.

Images.caption: On this southeast slope at close to 11,000 feet I was getting clean failures on a layer of rimed snowflakes down about 50cm. This layer was unreactive in Extended Column Tests. - CAIC Forecaster in RMNP on 3/3/26
Images.altText: snowpit

Images.caption: Several avalanches were observed around the East Portal of the Moffat Tunnel from the late February wind and snow.
Images.altText: recent avalacnhes

days.content:
Up to 10 inches of new snow is expected to fall with westerly winds. You can trigger avalanches in the new snow, but the main concern remains finding a shallow spot in the slab on a steep slope, and triggering a large avalanche. The most dangerous slopes face the north half of the compass.

Keep it simple during the storm by choosing low-angle planar slopes and avoiding likely trigger points, like the thinner margins of a slab, convex rollovers, and shallowly buried rocks.

Images.caption: An observation from February 26th highlights the importance of conservative terrain selection through an avalanche failure on a test slope (pictured) and a moderate propagating test result below treeline. Our snowpack structure remains horrible and while signs of instability like cracking and collapsing have decreased from last weekend, the consequence of getting caught in a slide has not
Images.altText: A snowmobile side hilling across a slope with a crack extending out from the snowmobile below treeline.

Images.caption: In general, western aspects have seen less avalanche activity than slopes tilting more north or east. An observer reported a slide on a western aspect in La Plata Canyon that looked a few days old. Warm weather keeps slabs soft, making it easier to impact weak layers below. Although less likely and generally smaller in size, you may be able to trigger avalanches in isolated shallow and steep terrain on aspects that face west.
Images.altText: A debris fan can be seen at the bottom of a tree covered slope

days.content:
Look for and avoid areas of drifted snow on steep slopes. Two pulses of intense snow with easterly winds increase the danger on Friday. Easterly winds mean you should look out for drifted snow in atypical places. You can easily trigger avalanches where you find more than about 10 inches of recently drifted snow. Avalanches can step down to buried weak layers and grow larger.

Spots below ridgelines and along gully walls are likely places to find drifted snow. If you see cracking or feel a collapse, those are obvious signs to avoid these slopes. Those obvious clues are decreasing, and you could trigger an avalanche without any warning.

You can find safer travel choices in wind-sheltered or lower-elevation terrain, where the new snow remains unconsolidated.

Images.caption: The rounded pillows of snow on the ridgeline highlight how wind-loaded this slope is. This was a natural avalanche on an easterly aspect.
Images.altText: A large avalanche

Images.caption: This snow profile highlights how weak the snowpack is. The bottom 15cm are large grain facets with a 50-60cm slab above.
Images.altText: A snow pit with a saw for measurement.

Images.caption: This smooth pillow of snow that has a crack running through it is a classic sign of wind-drifted snow. Avoid steep slopes that look like this.
Images.altText: A smooth pillow of snow with a crack in it.

days.content:
You can trigger a dangerous avalanche on steep slopes that face northwest through north to east. The most dangerous slopes are near and above treeline, generally above 10,000 feet. You can trigger a dangerous avalanche from a distance, so be aware of steep slopes above you and where you stop to rest or regroup. You can dramatically decrease your chances of being caught in an avalanche by avoiding slopes steeper than about 30 degrees on these aspects of concern, or move to south or west-facing slopes.

A few inches of snow on Friday won't raise the danger. Light easterly winds might drift snow onto typically scoured slopes.

Images.caption: This avalanche was triggered by snowmobilers traveling at the bottom of the slope last Saturday on the Grand Mesa. This is the kind of avalanche you can still trigger if you venture onto similar terrain.
Images.altText: An avalanche with snowmobile tracks below the slope

Images.caption: This natural avalanche ran late on February 18 or early on February 19 in the Flat Tops, highlighting the dangerous conditions where recent storm snow sits on a weak, sugary base.
Images.altText: A natural avalanche in flat light conditions.

Images.caption: This snowcat-triggered avalanche is a good example of the terrain where you could trigger a similar avalanche. Steep slopes on north through east aspects are the most suspect.
Images.altText: A small avalanche above a road

days.content:
Avalanche danger will increase throughout the day as snow piles up and lingers into Saturday.

Up to a foot of new snow is expected with westerly winds. You can trigger avalanches in the new snow, but the main concern remains finding a shallow spot in the slab on a steep slope and triggering a large avalanche. The most dangerous slopes face the north half of the compass and east.

Keep it simple during the storm by choosing low-angle planar slopes and avoiding likely trigger points, like the thinner margins of a slab, convex rollovers, and shallowly buried rocks.

Images.caption: This new natural avalanche is a weird one... it happened on March 3rd, with no obvious reason why it failed. This avalanche happened on an east-facing slope below treeline in Tempter, a closed backcountry run just outside the Telluride Ski Resort boundary. It is weird because there are no apparent weather factors that contributed to its release. Keep this in mind going forward: weird winters lead to weird avalanches. While stability is improving on many slopes, we can't rule out the odd, unexpected avalanche.
Images.altText: An aerial image of an avalanche on a treed slope.

Images.caption: This snowpit was dug east of Silverton on a west-facing slope at 11,800 feet. Here the slab is shallower and sitting above moist faceted grains. It failed on isolation in both an extended column test and a compression test. All signs still point to poor structure and a snowpack that is trending towards its Springtime form.
Images.altText: An extended column test with ECTV written in the snow.

Images.caption: This is our most recent human-triggered avalanche, which was triggered remotely from below on February 28. Luckily, everyone involved is ok. Poor structure, shallow conditions, and warm weather keep the possibility of impacting a weak layer and triggering a slide elevated. Avalanches can be triggered from a distance or from lower-angle terrain below.
Images.altText: A large avalanche with two fingers starting from just below a wind stripped ridgeline.

Images.caption: This avalanche was triggered from the top of the line right at the ridgeline on February 27th. It was triggered after one skier had already skied the majority of the line. The crown is deep (over 4 feet in places) and very well-connected. Luckily, nobody was injured. This is a good example of the connectivity and size of avalanches you can trigger right now. Avalanches are the clearest sign of instability and a sign that dangerous avalanche conditions remain.
Images.altText: Avalanches on a slope with a wide and deep crown line crossing rocks and terrain features.

days.content:
Look for and avoid areas of drifted snow on steep slopes. Two pulses of intense snow with easterly winds increase the danger on Friday. Easterly winds mean you should look out for drifted snow in atypical places. You can easily trigger avalanches where you find more than about 10 inches of recently drifted snow. Be especially cautious on cross-loaded slopes with buried weak layers. Avalanches can step down to buried weak layers and break widely across terrain features.

The most avalanche-prone slopes are steep, open slopes below corniced ridges. You may also be able to trigger an avalanche by traveling on flat slopes beneath steep terrain or on low-angle slopes that are adjacent to and connected with steeper slopes. Careful planning and conservative terrain selection are essential.

Images.caption: A snowmobiler triggered this large avalanche on March 4 after high-marking other steep slopes without incident. Jones Pass, east-facing above treeline slope.
Images.altText: avalanche

Images.caption: A rider triggered this large avalanche on Berthoud Pass on Wednesday. The rider was able to self-rescue. Northeast-facing
Images.altText: big avalanche

Images.caption: This very large avalanche near the ridge was triggered sympathetically to a smaller, remotely-triggered slide lower down in the trees in No Name Gulch near Loveland Pass on February 27. Look closely, and you can see the track that triggered the first slide, away from both of the slopes that avalanched.
Images.altText: Large avalanches

days.content: Expect the avalanche danger to rise throughout the day as periods of heavy snowfall and shifting winds add load to a variable snowpack. You might be able to trigger a smaller avalanche in freshly drifted snow, or a more massive and destructive one that breaks deep in the snowpack. Steep northerly- and east-facing slopes at higher elevations are the most dangerous. Given high uncertainty, choosing less risky, lower-angle terrain is the safest option.

Images.caption: Skiers remotely triggered this large Persistent Slab avalanche on a southeast-facing slope last weekend.
Images.altText: A broad avaalnche that gouged to near the ground below a cliff.

Images.caption: A recent natural avalanche in Capitol Creek that likely slid during last week's rain event.
Images.altText: An avalanche

Images.caption: This very large avalanche released naturally around February 25th above East Snowmass Creek.
Images.altText: A broad avalanche highlighted in the sun.

Images.caption: This avalanche was triggered from nearly 1000 feet away near Ashcroft in the Elk Mountains.
Images.altText: An avalanche

days.content: The most dangerous and likely places to trigger an avalanche are on northeast through east to southeast-facing slopes near ridgelines and at upper elevations. Dense slabs have formed on weak snow layers on these aspects around ridges and leeward terrain features. Use extra caution if you find more than 8 inches of new or drifted snow. You could possibly trigger a small avalanche in the new snow, or the added load could break deeper, creating a much larger slide. Seek out good snow in lower-angle terrain to lower your risk.

Images.caption: This photo of steep, wind-drifted, easterly-facing terrain south of Monarch Pass is a perfect example of where you could trigger a deadly avalanche.
Images.altText: Snow blowing off a ridgeline into steep terrain.

Images.caption: A pair of fresh avalanches ran between late afternoon on 2/23 and mid-morning 2/24 south of Crested Butte.
Images.altText: An avalanche

Images.caption: This recent natural avalanche is a good example of the dangerous snowpack we're dealing with at the moment. This problem will take time to heal, and avalanches of this size will be hard to survive.
Images.altText: A large avalalnche in an open bowl

days.content:
New snow and wind-drifting snow will increase your chances of triggering avalanches in the coming days. Avalanche danger will increase as new snow accumulates.

If you are out traveling in the backcountry and observe 8 inches or more of new snow, identify areas where winds are drifting the new snow into deeper drifts. If that drift sits on old snow below, these areas may be where you can trigger an avalanche on a steep slope. You are most likely to find this set-up above treeline on north, northeast, and east slopes.

Images.caption: With shallow snow conditions this year, wind slabs will most likely be found in shallow cross-loaded features in the Sangre de Cristo Range. Recent winds have drifted more snow onto north, northeast, and east slopes, but even south and west facing slopes hold cross-loaded features. West and south slopes, wind slabs will be more isolated and smaller in size. A small wind slab triggered on the 15th.
Images.altText: A crown line near a rock with shallow rocks and a snowboard in the frame on a steep piece of extreme terrain.

Images.caption: Recent snow filled in the usual wind-drifted features in the Sangre de Cristo Range. After a week of warm weather and winds, wind slabs have become stubborn to unreactive on most slopes. Very steep, terrain features with firm cross-loaded snow drifts remain the most hazardous.
Images.altText: A peak with drifted snow

days.content:
Avalanche danger will increase throughout the day as snow piles up and lingers into Saturday.

Over a foot of new snow is expected, with north winds driving the possibility of a Gorge event and heavy snowfall in the Cimarrons, along north 550, and Camp Bird Road. You can trigger avalanches in the new snow, but the main concern remains finding a shallow spot in the slab on a steep slope and triggering a larger avalanche that breaks deep in the snowpack. The most dangerous slopes face the north half of the compass and east.

Keep it simple during the storm by choosing low-angle slopes protected from the wind.

Images.caption: This new natural avalanche is a weird one... it happened on March 3rd, with no obvious reason why it failed. This avalanche happened on an east-facing slope below treeline in Tempter, a closed backcountry run just outside the Telluride Ski Resort boundary. It is weird because there are no apparent weather factors that contributed to its release. Keep this in mind going forward: weird winters lead to weird avalanches. While stability is improving on many slopes, we can't rule out the odd, unexpected avalanche.
Images.altText: An aerial image of an avalanche on a treed slope.

Images.caption: This snowpit was dug east of Silverton on a west-facing slope at 11,800 feet. Here the slab is shallower and sitting above moist faceted grains. It failed on isolation in both an extended column test and a compression test. All signs still point to poor structure and a snowpack that is trending towards its Springtime form.
Images.altText: An extended column test with ECTV written in the snow.

Images.caption: This is our most recent human-triggered avalanche, which was triggered remotely from below on February 28. Luckily, everyone involved is ok. Poor structure, shallow conditions, and warm weather keep the possibility of impacting a weak layer and triggering a slide elevated. Avalanches can be triggered from a distance or from lower-angle terrain below.
Images.altText: A large avalanche with two fingers starting from just below a wind stripped ridgeline.

Images.caption: This avalanche was triggered from the top of the line right at the ridgeline on February 27th. It was triggered after one skier had already skied the majority of the line. The crown is deep (over 4 feet in places) and very well-connected. Luckily, nobody was injured. This is a good example of the connectivity and size of avalanches you can trigger right now. Avalanches are the clearest sign of instability and a sign that dangerous avalanche conditions remain.
Images.altText: Avalanches on a slope with a wide and deep crown line crossing rocks and terrain features.

days.content:
You can trigger a small avalanche where you find more than about 6 inches of drifted snow. Look for snow to accumulate throughout the day on Friday. Easterly winds will drift new snow onto typically scoured slopes.

The bigger hazard is triggering a slide that's big enough to bury you. If you find the wrong spot on a slope, like an area with uneven snow coverage and large rocks protruding from the snowpack, you can trigger a slide that breaks on buried weak layers. Previous scoured north and northwest-facing slopes near the ridgetop fit this description, especially if they're crossloaded with new snow.

To lower your risk of avalanches, choose slopes with a consistently deep snowpack and no terrain traps. Look for and avoid drifted snow below steep ridges and in gullies.

Images.caption: An avalanche near Seedhouse Road on February 21.
Images.altText: avalanche

Images.caption:
This snowpit is from around February 26, but it shows a weak layer of snow capable of propagating a fracture. This layer is less reactive than it was a week ago, but it is something to evaluate before riding a steep slope.

North-facing, 10600 feet on Soda Mountain.
Images.altText: snowpit

days.content:
Up to 6 inches of new snow is expected to fall in this area with westerly winds. You can trigger avalanches in the new snow, but the main concern remains finding a shallow spot in the slab on a steep slope, and triggering a large avalanche. The most dangerous slopes face the north half of the compass.

Keep it simple during the storm by choosing low-angle planar slopes and avoiding likely trigger points, like the thinner margins of a slab, convex rollovers, and shallowly buried rocks.

Images.caption: Snow surfaces have been affected by wind and sun over the past few days. Looking up at a northeast slope near treeline on Coal Bank Pass you can see a rippled, textured surface. We suspect some of these horizontal ripples are old cracks that formed during peak instability during the storm. We did not observe any signs of instability while out on February 2nd.
Images.altText: A snow surface with some cracks in it.

Images.caption: An observation from February 26th highlights the importance of conservative terrain selection through an avalanche failure on a test slope (pictured) and a moderate propagating test result below treeline. Our snowpack structure remains horrible and while signs of instability like cracking and collapsing have decreased from last weekend, the consequence of getting caught in a slide has not
Images.altText: A snowmobile side hilling across a slope with a crack extending out from the snowmobile below treeline.

Images.caption: As observers bump out further into terrain, naturals from the February 11-21st storm cycle continue to be reported. The majority of avalanches failed on terrain that faces north through east. The areas that didn't avalanche on these same aspects are the slopes that carry the deepest snowpack sitting above a healthy and well established weak layer. They have the most potential for larger connected slides. East face near Taylor Lake, La Plata Mountains.
Images.altText: An avalanche with a crown over steep rocky area on a connected an open snow face.

Images.caption: In general, western aspects have seen less avalanche activity than slopes tilting more north or east. An observer reported a slide on a western aspect in La Plata Canyon that looked a few days old. Warm weather keeps slabs soft, making it easier to impact weak layers below. Although less likely and generally smaller in size, you may be able to trigger avalanches in isolated shallow and steep terrain on aspects that face west.
Images.altText: A debris fan can be seen at the bottom of a tree covered slope

days.content: Expect the avalanche danger to rise throughout the day as periods of heavy snowfall and shifting winds add load to a variable snowpack. You might be able to trigger a smaller avalanche in freshly drifted snow, or a more massive and destructive one that breaks deep in the snowpack. Steep northeast through east to southeast-facing slopes near ridgelines and at upper elevations are the most dangerous. Given high uncertainty, choosing less risky, lower-angle terrain is the safest option.

Images.caption: This photo of steep, wind-drifted, easterly-facing terrain south of Monarch Pass is a perfect example of where you could trigger a deadly avalanche.
Images.altText: Snow blowing off a ridgeline into steep terrain.

Images.caption: A pair of fresh avalanches ran between late afternoon on 2/23 and mid-morning 2/24 south of Crested Butte.
Images.altText: An avalanche

Images.caption: This recent natural avalanche is a good example of the dangerous snowpack we're dealing with at the moment. This problem will take time to heal, and avalanches of this size will be hard to survive.
Images.altText: A large avalalnche in an open bowl

days.content: Expect the avalanche danger to rise throughout the day as periods of heavy snowfall and shifting winds add load to a variable snowpack. You might be able to trigger a smaller avalanche in freshly drifted snow, or a more massive and destructive one that breaks deep in the snowpack. Steep northerly- and east-facing slopes are the most dangerous. Given high uncertainty, choosing less risky, lower-angle terrain is the safest option.

Images.caption: This avalanche was triggered by snowmobilers traveling at the bottom of the slope last Saturday on the Grand Mesa. This is the kind of avalanche you can still trigger if you venture onto similar terrain.
Images.altText: An avalanche with snowmobile tracks below the slope

Images.caption: This natural avalanche ran late on February 18 or early on February 19 in the Flat Tops, highlighting the dangerous conditions where recent storm snow sits on a weak, sugary base.
Images.altText: A natural avalanche in flat light conditions.

Images.caption: This snowcat-triggered avalanche is a good example of the terrain where you could trigger a similar avalanche. Steep slopes on north through east aspects are the most suspect.
Images.altText: A small avalanche above a road

days.content:
New snow will increase your chances of triggering a small avalanche, but it is not enough to increase the overall avalanche hazard.

If you are out traveling in the backcountry and observe 8 inches or more of new snow, identify areas where winds are drifting the new snow into deeper drifts. If that drift sits on old snow below, these areas may be where you can trigger an avalanche on a steep slope. You are most likely to find this set-up above treeline on north, northeast, and east slopes.

Images.caption: Warm weather and sun over the past week have fueled the rapid settlement of the mid-February snowfall. Water has already moved through most of the snowpack on south tilting slopes, decreasing the chance of wet avalanches. You can track the progress of meltwater by identifying percolation tubes like these ones pictured. Move off sunny slopes if you begin sinking deeply into wet, mushy snow.
Images.altText: A crystal card sits behind narrow percolation tubes in a snowpack

Images.caption: Recent snow filled in the usual wind-drifted features in the Sangre de Cristo Range. After a week of warm weather and winds, wind slabs have become stubborn to unreactive on most slopes. Very steep, terrain features with firm cross-loaded snow drifts remain the most hazardous. New snow on those firm drifts are where you might be able to trigger a small avalanche.
Images.altText: A peak with drifted snow

title: Southern Mountains Regional Discussion
message:
Southern Mountains Regional Discussion

A wacky Spring/Winter storm arrives overnight. Models have increased snow numbers throughout the day for this storm, with the most notable increase in the Uncompahgre Gorge, just south of Ouray, where north flow could fuel good snowfall and a possible "Gorge event" for that and the surrounding areas.

Total expected snowfall ranges from 6 to 20 inches, with the least amount of snow in the northeast and southwest San Juan Mountains, and the most amount of snow in the northwest and southeast San Juan and Sangre de Cristo Mountains. The wind direction starts out southwest, but by tomorrow, it moves more westerly and north, as the trough moves across the state. The storm starts warm but cools down as a cold front joins the party. At this point, we are not expecting any R@!#. So that's good news. Most of the snow will happen on Friday, but there will be some lingering snow on Friday night and into Saturday morning, especially for the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

Avalanche danger will rise during the day tomorrow as snow accumulates and peak in most areas, but with little known about how the new snow is affecting deeper weak layers, Conservative terrain choices and CONSIDERABLE (3 of 5) Danger will linger into the start of the weekend. Avalanche danger will be greatest in the areas that pick up over a foot of snow and less elsewhere. Winds will be strong enough to move snow, but not that strong, so drifting is expected to be confined to near ridgelines and cross-loaded terrain. The winds are also not loading the most dangerous slopes, but pay attention to deep drifts.

The new snow will fall on a tired snowpack, beaten up by recent warming and wind. You will be able to trigger avalanches in the new snow, but the main concern remains triggering a slide that breaks into old snow layers. Shallow trigger points remain the name of the game, and now will be even harder to identify. It has been some time since our last large or very large Persistent Slab avalanche, and as things were trending more stubbornly, but this load will once again be a test. One would think that Red flags will still be far and few between so avoidance is still likely the best practice for staying safe.

As you travel in the backcountry on Friday, pay attention to how much snow is falling and where the wind is driving that snow. Remember, it might be white on the surface, but shallow junk sits under the fresh coat of white. Enjoy the new snow by sticking to slopes with a steepness of less than 30 degrees.

Spring storms can sometimes be a bust; only time will tell with this one.

Images.caption: Propagating failures in snowpack tests highlight a slab's ability to move across space. The slab from the big storm in late February will be tested by the next loading event.
Images.altText: An Extended Column Test on a slope that looks warmed by the sun showing propagation.

Images.caption: This avalanche was triggered remotely from below on February 28th. It is a good example of the dangerous avalanche conditions that exist. While triggered in the northern San Juan Mountains, you can trigger similar slides in the Southern San Juan Mountains.
Images.altText: A large avalanche that starts above treeline and descend the length of the path below treeline

Images.caption: This is a screenshot from an awesome observation from the Pass Creek Road area, south of Wolf Creek Pass. The riders report signs of instability trending downward during the week, along with moderate propagating failures and an avalanche triggered on a test slope. This is a good example of the struggle of our Persistent Slab Problem in a lower-probability, high-consequence regime. Check out the observation (link in discussion) for videos of the slope test and pit tests.
Images.altText: A snowmobile side hills on a below treeline slope with a visible crack forming in front of the snowmobile

Images.caption: A strong over weak snowpack structure persists on many slopes. While you can trigger avalanches in the new snow, avalanches that step down deeper in the snowpack will be much more dangerous.
Images.altText: Snowpack with 1 finger over fist

Images.caption: Weird winters make for weird avalanches. And Persistent Slab avalanches can happen long after the storm has passed. This avalanche failed suddenly on a calm, sunny morning outside of Telluride, with no obvious weather factors contributing to it, two weeks after the storm. Don't let your guard down.
The most recent 2k WRF run shows the wide range of snow totals expected by Saturday morning.

title: Central Mountains Regional Discussion
message:
Central Mountains Regional Discussion

We elevated the danger to CONSIDERABLE (3of5) as forecasted storm totals jumped up in recent model runs. That said, there is still a lot of uncertainty amongst our forecast team, as juicier model runs haven't always worked out this season. Places that receive only 4 to 8 inches will probably get by with fewer problems, but in favored areas that push into double digits, things could deteriorate quickly. The other kicker is variability in wind direction as the low-pressure system passes through. This may change significantly from region to region, so keep an eye out for unusual loading patterns.

The one thing we do know is that the snowpack structure is not great to pretty terrible in many places, keeping the current Persistent Slab problem our top concern. With recent warm temperatures following the last loading event, slabs have settled and gained strength, but the weak snow holding them up has been slow to heal. With additional load, this could reactivate the problem, or at the very least, increase the size of the avalanche you can trigger. Regardless, it's not going to help in the short term. Managing this problem is pretty simple: just avoid being in or around avalanche terrain.

Depending on wind-drifting, you may also need to manage some fresh surface slabs. The winds look to be predominantly from the northwest, but could shift around to north or northeast. If you find a new slab on a southerly slope, it has less of a chance of breaking further down into buried weak layers and could be an isolated problem. However, if they build on a cross-loaded east- or northeast-facing slope, they could turn into a much bigger problem.

Bottom line, expect the danger to rise throughout the day, and adjust your plans if you run into higher snow totals, drifted slopes, or see obvious signs of instability.

Images.caption: This avalanche was triggered on the left side of the ridgeline and behind the knoll where you see tracks, over 1000 feet away from where the avalanche occurred. February 23, 2026, near Ashcroft in the Elk Mountains.
Images.altText: An avalanche with tracks nearby

Images.caption: A recent avalanche on Independence Pass in wind-drifted terrain above treeline. February 22, 2026
Images.altText: avalanche

Images.caption: A skier triggered this avalanche where you see tracks in West Willow Creek on February 21. The avalanche then sympathetically triggered 2 other avalanches hundreds of feet away.
Numerous avalanches were remotely triggered across the Grand Mesa by snowmobilers over the past few days.
Wet Slab avalanche activity in Capitol Creek after last week's rain-on-snow event.

title: Northern Mountains Regional Discussion
message:
Northern Mountains Regional Discussion

A short-lived storm approaches Colorado on Thursday night. Snow starts Friday morning and continues into Saturday. Look for widespread 6 to 10 inches, and favored areas could see a foot or more. With snow totals increasing over the last few weather model runs and favored areas varying between models, confidence is low in the specifics of where the highest totals will fall and what they’ll be. Northerly and easterly winds mean confidence is highest along the eastern Front Range and lower as you move further west. Areas from Rocky Mountain National Park south to Mount Blue Sky could see a foot of new snow. In the western Front Range, Vail, and Summit County areas, confidence is lower. If the moisture doesn’t make it over the Continental Divide, snow totals could be far lower than anticipated, closer to 4 to 6 inches. The Park Range and Elkhead Mountains mostly miss out on this one. Rabbit Ears Pass might see 6 to 8 inches, but it looks like 2 to 4 inches for the rest of these areas. The northern Flat Tops are looking like they’ll fall on the higher end of storm totals - close to a foot - by Saturday.

We can break the Northern Mountains into three distinct snowpack zones. The Park Range has the deepest snowpack, with buried weak layers that have strengthened over the past few weeks. A layer of facets remains buried about 2 feet deep, but there has been no recent evidence that this weak layer is reactive. The Park Range sits on the lower end of MODERATE (2 of 5) danger. With 6 inches of snow possible by the end of the day Friday, you might be able to trigger a small slide in newly drifted snow, but avoiding thin, variable slopes with trigger points is the best way to stay safe.

In the Elkhead Mountains, Flat Top Mountains, western and southern Front Range, and Gore Range, the snowpack is more concerning. We’re just coming off the most dangerous conditions of the season, so far, and more snow will exacerbate the issue. Since the Flat Tops, Front Range, and Gore Range are expected to receive a significant amount of new snow by Saturday morning, the danger increases to CONSIDERABLE (3 of 5). The Elkhead Mountains stay at MODERATE (2 of 5) with only a few inches in the forecast. Across these areas, weak layers are capped by cohesive slabs anywhere from 1 to 3 feet deep. Avalanche activity is slowing as buried weak layers adjust to the load and slabs continue to settle, but reports of two large slides in the past two days mean it hasn’t fully adjusted yet. With more snow on the way, look for more scary avalanches. You might just trigger a smaller slide in the new snow, but slides breaking across terrain features multiple feet deep are the bigger concern. Look for the new snow to drift into atypical places on Friday. Easterly winds mean cross-loaded slopes at the margins of our Persistent Slab problem might become more dangerous faster than typically loaded slopes.

Lastly, we have the east side of Rocky Mountain National Park and the Indian Peaks. Forecasters visited these zones numerous times and found no evidence of concerning persistent weak layers. In this region, avalanche danger is primarily driven by new snow and wind. A foot of new snow means the danger is increasing. Winds aren’t particularly strong, so you might find more of a Loose Dry rather than a Wind Slab avalanche problem. Either way, steep slopes below ridges and in gullies and couloirs are the most likely places to trigger slides in the new snow. With weak surfaces observed before this storm, look out for small slides that run fast and far. Make sure you know what’s above and below you.

Images.caption: This large avalanche was triggered on February 25 near the summit of Berthoud Pass. People have been riding this slope all winter with little issue, but that didn't matter last Wednesday when a new slab formed. You should move carefully in the mountains and avoid avalanches by not being on or under steep slopes.
Images.altText: a large avalanche below a rocky band.

Images.caption: This large avalanche near Loveland Pass was triggered from a distance on February 27. This is a great example of the size and scale of the avalanche you can trigger.
Images.altText: Large avalanche below a ridge crest with snow blowing off the peaks.

Images.caption: These riders were well away from steep slopes and were able to trigger a massive avalanche. This highlights how careful you need to be around steep slopes.
Images.altText: A partially buried snow mobile in avalanche debris.
